---
title: "mini project"
output: word_document
date: "2026-02-28"
---
```{r}
#here we are importing the dataset and loading it in the r 
data <- read.csv("C:/Users/DAMAN/Downloads/r programming project-2.csv")
data
#we are changing names of the column here to make it more understanding about the data 
names(data)
names(data) <- c(
  "ProductType",
  "SKU",
  "Price",
  "Availability",
  "ProductsSold",
  "Revenue",
  "ProductionVolume",
  "LeadTime",
  "ManufacturingCost"
)
View(data)
#we are performing basic statistical measures 
names(data)
mean(data$Price,na.rm=T)
mode(data$Price)
var(data$Price)
sd(data$Price)
quantile(data$Price)
summary(data)
str(data)



#frequency distribution of the prices 
table(cut(data$Price,breaks=c(0,20,40,60,80,100)))
table(data$Price)
data$PriceCategory <- ifelse(data$Price < 50, "Low",
                       ifelse(data$Price < 100, "Medium",
                       ifelse(data$Price < 200, "High")))

#histogram of booth revenue and price
hist(data$Price,
     col="red",
     main="price and manufacturing cost histogram",
     xlab = "values")
hist(data$ManufacturingCost,
     col="blue",
     add=T)
#scatter plot for the revenue and price
boxplot(data$Price,
     data$Revenue,
     main="box plot for revenue and price",
     xlab="price",
     ylab="revenue",
     pch=19,
     col="yellow")


#“Analysis of Product Price Distribution in Manufacturing & Production using ggplot”
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data,aes(x=Price))+
  geom_histogram(bins=10)+
  ggtitle("distribution of product price in manufacturing and production")+
  xlab("price")+
  ylab("frequency")

#boxplot for manufacturing cost using ggplot
ggplot(data,aes(y=ManufacturingCost))+
  geom_boxplot(fill="skyblue",color="darkblue")+
  ggtitle("box plot of manufacturing cost")+
  xlab("price")+
  ylab("frequnecy")

#bivariate analysis and getting regression line 
# create regression model FIRST
model <- lm(Price ~ ManufacturingCost, data = data)

# scatter plot
plot(data$ManufacturingCost,
     data$Price,
     main="Price vs Manufacturing Cost",
     xlab="Manufacturing Cost",
     ylab="Price",
     col="blue",
     pch=19)

# regression line
abline(model, col="red", lwd=2)

#correlation between price and manufacturing cost 
cor(data$Price,data$ManufacturingCost)

#use of lapply and sapply
sapply(data[,3:9],mean,na.rm=T)
lapply(data[,3:9],summary,na.rm=T)

#attach and detach function
attach(data)
range(data$Revenue,na.rm = T)
min(data$Revenue,na.rm=T)
max(data$Revenue,na.rm=T)
detach(data)

#dataframe implementation
df <- data.frame(
  ProductType = c("skincare","haircare"),
  Price = c(61.1, 42.3),
  Revenue = c(7766, 5000)
)
df









```
Conclusion

The Manufacturing & Production dataset was analyzed using R programming to understand product pricing and production characteristics. Both numerical and categorical variables were identified and basic statistical measures such as mean, variance, and standard deviation were calculated. Visualizations including histograms, boxplots, and scatter plots helped in understanding data distribution and relationships between variables. Bivariate analysis showed the relationship between price and manufacturing cost, while multivariate functions were used to summarize multiple variables efficiently. Overall, the analysis demonstrated how R can be used for effective data analysis and visualization.
